revision.text:
The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in Japan from the [[Kamakura Period]] to the [[Edo Period]]. It descended from a clan of [[Seiwa Genji]], who was a direct descendent of [[Emperor Seiwa]]. From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan was the protector of [[Mino Province]]. At its greatest power, it controlled three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].
[[zh:土岐氏]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[Category:Japanese clans]]

revision.comment: added sources/information
revision.text:
The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in Japan from the [[Kamakura Period]] to the [[Edo Period]]. It descended from a clan of [[Seiwa Genji]], who was a direct descendent of [[Emperor Seiwa]]. From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan was the protector of [[Mino Province]]. At its greatest power, it controlled three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref>
The family used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]], [[Gifu Prefecture]] as their hometown<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> and adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including Shōbō-ji Temple<ref name="sengokuexpo"/> in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]].
==References== <references/>
[[zh:土岐氏]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[Category:Japanese clans]]

revision.text:
The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in Japan from the [[Kamakura Period]] to the [[Edo Period]]. It descended from a clan of [[Seiwa Genji]], who was a direct descendent of [[Emperor Seiwa]]. From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan was the protector of [[Mino Province]]. At its greatest power, it controlled three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref>
The family used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]], [[Gifu Prefecture]] as their hometown<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> and adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including Shōbō-ji Temple<ref name="sengokuexpo"/> in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]].
==References== <references/>
[[Category:Japanese clans]]
[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in Japan from the [[Kamakura Period]] to the [[Edo Period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] (850-880) by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] 944-1021) from the [[minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji).
Minamoto no Mitsunobu, descended from Yorimitsu in the 4th generation, settled at Toki (Mino province) and took the name of that place.
Toki Yorisada, was the grandson of [[Hojo Sadatoki]] (''Skikken of Kamakura'') on his mother's side. He fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan ruled [[Mino Province]]. Toki Yasuyuki governed three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''Shogun'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought during two years (1389-1391).
Toki Nariyori (+ 1497), during the Onin war sided with the [[Yamana clan]], and in 1487 invaded the southern part of Omi province.
The family used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]], [[Gifu Prefecture]] as their hometown<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> and adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including Shōbō-ji Temple<ref name="sengokuexpo"/> in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]].
During the 16th century, the principal line of the Toki lost their possessions during the civil wars that desolated Mino province.
Sadamasa (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two, and was carried to Mikawa province. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army, was created in 1590 ''[[Daimyo]]'' of Soma ((Shimosa province), and revived the glory of the Toki family. In 1868, his descendants were still ''Daimyo'' of Numata (Kozuke province).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches : the [[Asano]], the [[Akechi]], and the [[Takenaka]] clans. ==References== <references/>
[[Category:Japanese clans]]
[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

Minamoto no Mitsunobu, descendant of Yorimitsu in the 4th generation, settled at Toki (Mino province) and took the name of that place.
Toki Yorisada, was the grandson of [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[Shikken]]'' of Kamakura on his mother's side. He fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan ruled [[Mino Province]]. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''Shogun'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought during two years (1389-1391).
Toki Nariyori (+ 1497), during the Ōnin war sided with the [[Yamana clan]], and in 1487 invaded the southern part of Ōmi province.

Sadamasa (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two, and was carried to Mikawa province. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army, was created in 1590 ''[[Daimyo]]'' of Sōma ((Shimōsa province), and revived the glory of the Toki family. In 1868, his descendants were still ''Daimyo'' of Numata (Kōzuke province).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches : the [[Asano]], the [[Akechi]], and the [[Takenaka]] clans. ==References== <references/>

[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in Japan from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] (850-880) by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] 944-1021) from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji).
Minamoto no Mitsunobu, descendant of Yorimitsu in the 4th generation, settled at Toki (Mino province) and took the name of that place.
Toki Yorisada, was the grandson of [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of Kamakura on his mother's side. He fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi Period]] to the [[Sengoku period|Warring States period]], the Toki clan ruled [[Mino Province]]. Toki Yasuyuki was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of three provinces : Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''shogun'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought during two years (1389-1391).
Toki Nariyori (+ 1497), during the Ōnin war sided with the [[Yamana clan]], and in 1487 invaded the southern part of Ōmi province.
The family used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]], [[Gifu Prefecture]] as their hometown<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> and adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including Shōbō-ji Temple<ref name="sengokuexpo"/> in [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]].
The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that desolated Mino province, when Toki Yoshiyori (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]] (1494-1556).
Sadamasa (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two, and was carried to Mikawa province. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army, was created in 1590 ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ((Shimōsa province), and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868 athe en of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata (Kōzuke province).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches : the [[Asano clan|Asano]], the [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], and the [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans. ==References== <references/>
[[Category:Japanese clans]]
[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Nariyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]].
The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].
[[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' of three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought during two years (1389-1391).
[[Toki Nariyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]].
The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].
[[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==References== <references/>

[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)

revision.comment: /* Clan rulers */
revision.text:
{{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in [[Japan]] from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji)<ref name="kenhaku">[http://www.museum.pref.gifu.jp/exhibition/arts_01/arts_01_19_dat.html Jinbun Tenji-shitsu]. {{ja icon}} Gifu Prefectural Museum. Accessed May 8, 2008.</ref> and used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] in [[Mino Province]] as their hometown.<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> The family adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]], and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
==History== [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was the first to settle in Toki. It was he who named and started the Toki clan. [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389-1391).
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]]. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551-1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. Below is a list of rulers of the Toki clan. The first five rulers resided in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. TThe second seven rulers were just heads of the clan resided in Toki; however, starting with Toki Yorisada, the rulers also served as the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancesters=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th son of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) ([[1271]]–[[1339]]) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) ([[1318]]–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died [[1397]]) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) ([[1351]]–[[1414]]) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) ([[1406]]–[[1474]]) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) ([[1442]]–[[1497]]) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) ([[1457]]–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) ([[1488]]–[[1536]]) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) ([[1502]]–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) ([[1524]]–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==References== <references/>

[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|right|thumb|150px|The Toki clan's crest]] {{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in [[Japan]] from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji)<ref name="kenhaku">[http://www.museum.pref.gifu.jp/exhibition/arts_01/arts_01_19_dat.html Jinbun Tenji-shitsu]. {{ja icon}} Gifu Prefectural Museum. Accessed May 8, 2008.</ref> and used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] in [[Mino Province]] as their hometown.<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> The family adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]], and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
==History== [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was the first to settle in Toki. It was he who named and started the Toki clan. [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]]. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. Below is a list of rulers of the Toki clan. The first six rulers resided in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The second seven rulers were just heads of the clan resided in Toki; however, starting with Toki Yorisada, the rulers also served as the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancesters=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) ([[1271]]–[[1339]]) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) ([[1318]]–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died [[1397]]) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) ([[1351]]–[[1414]]) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) ([[1406]]–[[1474]]) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) ([[1442]]–[[1497]]) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) ([[1457]]–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) ([[1488]]–[[1536]]) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) ([[1502]]–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) ([[1524]]–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==References== <references/>
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]
[[fr:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[zh:土岐氏]]

In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. Below is a list of rulers of the Toki clan. The first six rulers resided in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The second seven rulers were just heads of the clan resided in Toki; however, starting with Toki Yorisada, the rulers also served as the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancesters=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) ([[1271]]–[[1339]]) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) ([[1318]]–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died [[1397]]) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) ([[1351]]–[[1414]]) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) ([[1406]]–[[1474]]) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) ([[1442]]–[[1497]]) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) ([[1457]]–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) ([[1488]]–[[1536]]) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) ([[1502]]–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) ([[1524]]–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==Famous Profiles== # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) a basara daimyo, attacked Kogen Joko by arrows. # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) the last shugo daimyo driven out by Saito Dosan

revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|right|thumb|150px|The Toki clan's crest]] {{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in [[Japan]] from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji)<ref name="kenhaku">[http://www.museum.pref.gifu.jp/exhibition/arts_01/arts_01_19_dat.html Jinbun Tenji-shitsu]. {{ja icon}} Gifu Prefectural Museum. Accessed May 8, 2008.</ref> and used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] in [[Mino Province]] as their hometown.<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> The family adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]], and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
==History== [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was the first to settle in Toki. It was he who named and started the Toki clan. [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]]. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) ([[1271]]–[[1339]]) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) ([[1318]]–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died [[1397]]) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) ([[1351]]–[[1414]]) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) ([[1406]]–[[1474]]) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) ([[1442]]–[[1497]]) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) ([[1457]]–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) ([[1488]]–[[1536]]) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) ([[1502]]–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) ([[1524]]–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==Famous Profiles== # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) a basara daimyo, attacked Kogen Joko by arrows. # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) the last shugo daimyo driven out by Saito Dosan

revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|right|thumb|150px|The Toki clan's crest]] {{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in [[Japan]] from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji)<ref name="kenhaku">[http://www.museum.pref.gifu.jp/exhibition/arts_01/arts_01_19_dat.html Jinbun Tenji-shitsu]. {{ja icon}} Gifu Prefectural Museum. Accessed May 8, 2008.</ref> and used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] in [[Mino Province]] as their hometown.<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> The family adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]], and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
==History== [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was the first to settle in Toki. It was he who named and started the Toki clan. [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]]. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. Below is a list of rulers of the Toki clan. The first six rulers resided in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The second seven rulers were just heads of the clan resided in Toki; however, starting with Toki Yorisada, the rulers also served as the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) ([[1271]]–[[1339]]) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) ([[1318]]–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died [[1397]]) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) ([[1351]]–[[1414]]) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) ([[1406]]–[[1474]]) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) ([[1442]]–[[1497]]) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) ([[1457]]–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) ([[1488]]–[[1536]]) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) ([[1502]]–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) ([[1524]]–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==Famous Profiles== # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) a basara daimyo, attacked Kogen Joko by arrows. # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) the last shugo daimyo driven out by Saito Dosan
==References== <references/>

===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==Famous Profiles== # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) a basara daimyo, attacked Kogen Joko by arrows. # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) the last shugo daimyo driven out by Saito Dosan

revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|thumb|right|140px|The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan]] {{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} {{Japanese name|Toki}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} was a powerful clan that ruled in [[Japan]] from the [[Kamakura period]] to the [[Edo period]]. It descended from [[Emperor Seiwa]] by [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] from the [[Minamoto clan]] (Seiwa genji)<ref name="kenhaku">[http://www.museum.pref.gifu.jp/exhibition/arts_01/arts_01_19_dat.html Jinbun Tenji-shitsu]. {{ja icon}} Gifu Prefectural Museum. Accessed May 8, 2008.</ref> and used [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] in [[Mino Province]] as their hometown.<ref name="tokishi">[http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html Toki City The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware]. Toki City Hall. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> The family adhered to Zen Buddhist beliefs and founded many temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]], which contains the [[Gifu Great Buddha]], and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
==History== [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was the first to settle in Toki. It was he who named and started the Toki clan. [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of three provinces: Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="sengokuexpo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html Toki clan]. Sengoku Expo. Accessed July 4, 2007.</ref> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province, when [[Toki Yoshiyori]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]]. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) lost his father at the age of two and was carried to [[Mikawa Province]]. At the age of 14 he entered in [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named ''[[daimyo]]'' of Sōma ([[Shimōsa Province]]) and revived the former glory of the Toki. In 1868, at the end of the [[Tokugawa period]], his descendants were ''daimyo'' of Numata ([[Kōzuke Province]]).
The Toki are also famous for their cadet branches: the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Clan rulers== The Toki clan, originating from Emperor Seiwa, made powerful samurai core families from generations to generations known as [[Seiwa Genji]]. Below is a list of rulers of the Toki clan. The first six rulers resided in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The second seven rulers were just heads of the clan resided in Toki; however, starting with Toki Yorisada, the rulers also served as the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) - founder of the Toki clan and the family crest # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==Famous Profiles== # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) a basara daimyo, attacked Kogen Joko by arrows. # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) the last shugo daimyo driven out by Saito Dosan
==References== <references/>
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

revision.comment: cite support
revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|thumb|right|140px|The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan]] {{Cleanup|date=December 2007}} {{Japanese name|Toki}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.<ref name="papinot61">[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9.</ref>
==History== The Toki claims descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa genji]].<ref name="papinot61"/>
As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muramachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.<ref name="tokishi">Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref>
The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]]<ref name="expo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref> and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
[[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name<ref name="papinot61"/> [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact}}
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="expo"/> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact}}
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].<ref name="nussbaum809">[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809.</ref>
[[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]).<ref name="papinot61"/>
===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. Thenext seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)

revision.comment: remvoe 2007 headnote
revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|thumb|right|140px|The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan]] {{Japanese name|Toki}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.<ref name="papinot61">[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9.</ref>
==History== The Toki claims descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa genji]].<ref name="papinot61"/>
As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muramachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.<ref name="tokishi">Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref>
The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]]<ref name="expo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref> and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
[[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name<ref name="papinot61"/> [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact}}
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="expo"/> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact}}
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].<ref name="nussbaum809">[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809.</ref>
[[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]).<ref name="papinot61"/>
===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], Hida, Ikeda, [[Akechi clan|Akechi]] and [[Takenaka clan|Takenaka]] clans.
==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. Thenext seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)

revision.comment: Dating maintenance tags: {{Fact}}
revision.text:
[[Image:Tokikikyo.svg|thumb|right|140px|The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan]] {{Japanese name|Toki}} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.<ref name="papinot61">[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9.</ref>
==History== The Toki claims descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa genji]].<ref name="papinot61"/>
As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muramachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.<ref>Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref>
The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]]<ref name="expo">[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10.</ref> and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]].
[[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name<ref name="papinot61"/> [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact|date=May 2013}}
From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]].<ref name="expo"/> When the ''[[shogun]]'' had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}}
[[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].<ref name="nussbaum809">[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809.</ref>
[[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]).<ref name="papinot61"/>
===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]] et [[Funaki clan|Funaki]].<ref name="papinot61"/>
==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. Thenext seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province.
===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）- the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Oh （経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) - Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）- Chinjufu Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国)
===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定)
===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice)
==References== {{reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）-　the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto　Oh　（経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲)　-　Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）-　 Chinjufu　Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

{{Japanese name|Toki}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = Image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province =[[Mino Province|Mino]] |parent house =[[Seiwa Genji]] |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches =[[Asano clan]]
[[Akechi clan]]
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Twara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claims descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa genji]]. As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muromachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact|date=May 2013}} From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[shogun]]'' [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}} [[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]] and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino Ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）-　the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto　Oh　（経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲)　-　Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）-　 Chinjufu　Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], [[Fumizuki clan| Fumizuki]] and[[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）-　the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto　Oh　（経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲)　-　Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）-　 Chinjufu　Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

{{Japanese name|Toki}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = Image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province =[[Mino Province|Mino]] |parent house =[[Seiwa Genji]] |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches =[[Asano clan]]
[[Akechi clan]]
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Twara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Fumizuki clan]]
[[Funaki clan]] }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claims descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa genji]]. As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muromachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact|date=May 2013}} From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[shogun]]'' [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}} [[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [http://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], [[Fumizuki clan| Fumizuki]] and[[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）-　the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto　Oh　（経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲)　-　Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）-　 Chinjufu　Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

When ''[[shogun]]'' [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}} [[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], [[Fumizuki clan |Fumizuki]] and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）-　the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto　Oh　（経基王）- the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲)　-　Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raiko) (源頼光）-　 Chinjufu　Shogun, famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318–February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457–September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502–December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524–December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muromachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"]; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact|date=May 2013}} From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[shōgun]]'' [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}} [[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], [[Fumizuki clan |Fumizuki]] and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）– the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王）– the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heas were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）– the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王）– the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]

{{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = Image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province =[[Mino Province|Mino]] |parent house =[[Seiwa Genji]] |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches =[[Asano clan]]
[[Akechi clan]]
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Twara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a [[Japan]]ese kin group.[[Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[Seiwa Genji]]. As governors of [[Mino Province]] during the [[Muromachi period]], [[Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[Gifu, Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[shikken]]'' of the [[Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[Ashikaga Takauji]].{{fact|date=May 2013}} From the [[Muromachi period]] to the [[Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[Owari Province|Owari]] and [[Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[shōgun]]'' [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391).{{fact|date=May 2013}} [[Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[Yamana clan]] during the [[Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[Saitō Dōsan]].[[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[koku]]'') in [[Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[Tanaka Domain]] in [[Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[Asano clan|Asano]], [[Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (清和天皇）– the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王） # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王）– the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]]
