revision.text:
[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q687635]] Wikidata name: Toki clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province =[[:en:Mino Province|Mino]] |parent house =[[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches =[[:en:Asano clan|Asano clan]]
[[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi clan]]
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. As governors of [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[:en:Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[:en:Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[:en:Hōjō Sadatoki|Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[:en:Shikken|Shikken]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. From the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[:en:Yamana clan|Yamana clan]] during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[:en:Saitō Dōsan|Saitō Dōsan]].[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] }} [[en:Toki clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}}

In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] }} [[en:Toki clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}}

Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. As governors of [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[:en:Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[:en:Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[:en:Hōjō Sadatoki|Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[:en:Shikken|Shikken]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. From the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[:en:Yamana clan|Yamana clan]] during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[:en:Saitō Dōsan|Saitō Dōsan]].[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] }} [[en:Toki clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}}

''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. As governors of [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[:en:Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[:en:Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[:en:Hōjō Sadatoki|Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[:en:Shikken|Shikken]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. From the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[:en:Yamana clan|Yamana clan]] during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[:en:Saitō Dōsan|Saitō Dōsan]].[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] }} [[en:Toki clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q687635]] Wikidata name: Toki clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province =[[:en:Mino Province|Mino]] |parent house =[[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]] |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches =[[:en:Asano clan|Asano clan]]
[[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi clan]]
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] and the [[:en:Seiwa Genji|Seiwa Genji]]. As governors of [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[:en:Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[:en:Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[:en:Hōjō Sadatoki|Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[:en:Shikken|Shikken]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. From the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[:en:Yamana clan|Yamana clan]] during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[:en:Saitō Dōsan|Saitō Dōsan]].[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

As governors of [[:en:Mino Province|Mino Province]] during the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], [[:en:Toki, Gifu|Toki]] was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including [[:en:Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|Shōhō-ji]][http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and [[:en:Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|Sōfuku-ji]] in the city of [[:en:Gifu|Gifu]]. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was [[:en:Hōjō Sadatoki|Hōjō Sadatoki]], ''[[:en:Shikken|Shikken]]'' of the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]], fought against the Southern Dynasty with [[:en:Ashikaga Takauji|Ashikaga Takauji]]. From the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' (governor) of Mino, [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Ise Province|Ise]]. When ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]'' [[:en:Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] sided with the [[:en:Yamana clan|Yamana clan]] during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of [[:en:Ōmi Province|Ōmi Province]]. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (then governor of Mino) was defeated by [[:en:Saitō Dōsan|Saitō Dōsan]].[[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]]. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]]'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'') in [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimōsa Province]]). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to [[:en:Takatsuki Domain|Takatsuki Domain]] (30,000 ''koku'') in [[:en:Settsu Province|Settsu Province]]. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to [[:en:Kaminoyama Domain|Kaminoyama Domain]] in [[:en:Dewa Province|Dewa Province]]; in 1712 to [[:en:Tanaka Domain|Tanaka Domain]] in [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in [[:en:Numata Domain|Numata Domain]] (35,000 ''koku'') in ([[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the [[:en:Asano clan|Asano]], [[:en:Akechi clan|Akechi]], [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # [[:en:Minamoto no Yorimitsu|Minamoto no Yorimitsu]] (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # [[:en:Toki Shigeyori|Toki Shigeyori]] (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # [[:en:Toki Masafusa|Toki Masafusa]] (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # [[:en:Toki Yorinari|Toki Yorinari]] (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

revision.text:
[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q687635]] Wikidata name: Toki clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province ={{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}} |parent house ={{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches ={{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano clan}}
{{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi clan}}
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. As governors of {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Toki, Gifu|en|Toki, Gifu|WD=Q847332|lt=Toki}} was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including {{ill|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3482779|lt=Shōhō-ji}}[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and {{ill|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|en|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3511679|lt=Sōfuku-ji}} in the city of {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was {{ill|Hōjō Sadatoki|en|Hōjō Sadatoki|WD=Q3136681|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, fought against the Southern Dynasty with {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. From the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' (governor) of Mino, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise}}. When ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} sided with the {{ill|Yamana clan|en|Yamana clan|WD=Q2975406|lt=Yamana clan}} during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (then governor of Mino) was defeated by {{ill|Saitō Dōsan|en|Saitō Dōsan|WD=Q1202670|lt=Saitō Dōsan}}.{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'') in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to {{ill|Takatsuki Domain|en|Takatsuki Domain|WD=Q10863415|lt=Takatsuki Domain}} (30,000 ''koku'') in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to {{ill|Kaminoyama Domain|en|Kaminoyama Domain|WD=Q847283|lt=Kaminoyama Domain}} in {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province}}; in 1712 to {{ill|Tanaka Domain|en|Tanaka Domain|WD=Q420800|lt=Tanaka Domain}} in {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in {{ill|Numata Domain|en|Numata Domain|WD=Q10863454|lt=Numata Domain}} (35,000 ''koku'') in ({{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the {{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano}}, {{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi}}, [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

revision.text:
[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q687635]] Wikidata name: Toki clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province ={{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}} |parent house ={{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches ={{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano clan}}
{{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi clan}}
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. As governors of {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Toki, Gifu|en|Toki, Gifu|WD=Q847332|lt=Toki}} was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including {{ill|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3482779|lt=Shōhō-ji}}[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and {{ill|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|en|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3511679|lt=Sōfuku-ji}} in the city of {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was {{ill|Hōjō Sadatoki|en|Hōjō Sadatoki|WD=Q3136681|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, fought against the Southern Dynasty with {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. From the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' (governor) of Mino, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise}}. When ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} sided with the {{ill|Yamana clan|en|Yamana clan|WD=Q2975406|lt=Yamana clan}} during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (then governor of Mino) was defeated by {{ill|Saitō Dōsan|en|Saitō Dōsan|WD=Q1202670|lt=Saitō Dōsan}}.{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'') in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to {{ill|Takatsuki Domain|en|Takatsuki Domain|WD=Q10863415|lt=Takatsuki Domain}} (30,000 ''koku'') in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to {{ill|Kaminoyama Domain|en|Kaminoyama Domain|WD=Q847283|lt=Kaminoyama Domain}} in {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province}}; in 1712 to {{ill|Tanaka Domain|en|Tanaka Domain|WD=Q420800|lt=Tanaka Domain}} in {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in {{ill|Numata Domain|en|Numata Domain|WD=Q10863454|lt=Numata Domain}} (35,000 ''koku'') in ({{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the {{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano}}, {{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi}}, [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province ={{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino}} |parent house ={{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}} |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches ={{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano clan}}
{{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi clan}}
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji}}. As governors of {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province}} during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Toki, Gifu|en|Toki, Gifu|WD=Q847332|lt=Toki}} was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including {{ill|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3482779|lt=Shōhō-ji}}[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and {{ill|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|en|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3511679|lt=Sōfuku-ji}} in the city of {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu}}. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was {{ill|Hōjō Sadatoki|en|Hōjō Sadatoki|WD=Q3136681|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}}, fought against the Southern Dynasty with {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji}}. From the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' (governor) of Mino, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari}} and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise}}. When ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} sided with the {{ill|Yamana clan|en|Yamana clan|WD=Q2975406|lt=Yamana clan}} during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}}. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (then governor of Mino) was defeated by {{ill|Saitō Dōsan|en|Saitō Dōsan|WD=Q1202670|lt=Saitō Dōsan}}.{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'') in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province}}). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to {{ill|Takatsuki Domain|en|Takatsuki Domain|WD=Q10863415|lt=Takatsuki Domain}} (30,000 ''koku'') in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province}}. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to {{ill|Kaminoyama Domain|en|Kaminoyama Domain|WD=Q847283|lt=Kaminoyama Domain}} in {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province}}; in 1712 to {{ill|Tanaka Domain|en|Tanaka Domain|WD=Q420800|lt=Tanaka Domain}} in {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in {{ill|Numata Domain|en|Numata Domain|WD=Q10863454|lt=Numata Domain}} (35,000 ''koku'') in ({{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the {{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano}}, {{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi}}, [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[sr:Клан Токи]][[zh:土岐氏]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]][[en:Toki clan]][[es:Clan toki]][[fa:خاندان توکی]][[fr:Clan Toki]][[it:Clan Toki]][[ja:土岐氏]][[pt:Clã Toki]][[ru:Токи (клан)]][[zh:土岐氏]] {{Short description|Samurai clan}} {{family name hatnote|Toki|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 土岐氏 |image = image:Tokikikyo.svg |image size = 140px |image caption = The emblem ([[:en:Mon (badge)|''mon'']]) of the Toki clan |home province ={{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino|1=Mino Province}} |parent house ={{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} |titles =Various |founder =[[Toki Yorisada]] (Minamoto no Mitsunobu) |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = |dissolution = |ruled until = |cadet branches ={{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano clan|1=Asano clan}}
{{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi clan|1=Akechi clan}}
[[Seyasu clan]]
[[Ibi clan]]
[[Hidase clan]]
[[Osu clan]]
[[Tawara clan]]
[[Toyama clan]]
[[Funaki clan]]
Fumizuki clan }} The {{Nihongo|'''Toki clan'''|土岐氏|''Toki-shi''}} is a Japanese kin group.[[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph]]. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d’histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Toki," ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 61]; retrieved 2013-5-9. ==History== The Toki claim descent from {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} and the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}}. As governors of {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|WD=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}} during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Toki, Gifu|en|Toki, Gifu|WD=Q847332|lt=Toki|1=Toki, Gifu}} was the seat of the Toki clan.Toki City, [http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html "The Historical and Geographical Background of Mino Ware"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070717085948/http://www.city.toki.lg.jp/wcore/english/mino/historical.html |date=2007-07-17 }}; retrieved 2013-5-10. The Toki founded Zen Buddhist temples, including {{ill|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|en|Shōhō-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3482779|lt=Shōhō-ji|1=Shōhō-ji (Gifu)}}[http://www.sengoku-expo.net/person/E/053.html "Toki clan" at Sengoku-expo.net]; retrieved 2013-5-10. and {{ill|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|en|Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)|WD=Q3511679|lt=Sōfuku-ji|1=Sōfuku-ji (Gifu)}} in the city of {{ill|Gifu|en|Gifu|WD=Q45798|lt=Gifu|1=Gifu}}. [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]], a fourth generation descendant of Yorimitsu, was installed in Toki; and he took the name [[Toki Yorisada]], whose maternal grandfather was {{ill|Hōjō Sadatoki|en|Hōjō Sadatoki|WD=Q3136681|lt=Hōjō Sadatoki|1=Hōjō Sadatoki}}, ''{{ill|Shikken|en|Shikken|WD=Q1045904|lt=Shikken|1=Shikken}}'' of the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate|1=Kamakura shogunate}}, fought against the Southern Dynasty with {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}. From the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, the Toki clan ruled Mino Province. [[Toki Yasuyuki]] was ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' (governor) of Mino, {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari|1=Owari Province}} and {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise|1=Ise Province}}. When ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}'' {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} had tried to take Owari from him, Yasuyuki refused and fought for two years (1389–1391). {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori|1=Toki Shigeyori}} sided with the {{ill|Yamana clan|en|Yamana clan|WD=Q2975406|lt=Yamana clan|1=Yamana clan}} during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} and, in 1487, invaded the southern part of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}. The principal line of the Toki lost their possessions in 1542 during the civil wars that decimated Mino Province. {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari|1=Toki Yorinari}} (then governor of Mino) was defeated by {{ill|Saitō Dōsan|en|Saitō Dōsan|WD=Q1202670|lt=Saitō Dōsan|1=Saitō Dōsan}}.{{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|WD=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA809&dq= "Saitō Dōsan"] in ''Japan Encyclopedia'', p. 809. [[Toki Sadamasa]] (1551–1597) earned distinction fighting in the forces of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}'s army. In 1590, he was named head of [[:en:Sōma Domain|Sōma Domain]] (10,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'') in {{ill|Shimōsa Province|en|Shimōsa Province|WD=Q1053695|lt=Shimōsa Province|1=Shimōsa Province}}). Sadamasa's son [[Toki Sadayoshi]] (1579–1618) was moved in 1617 to {{ill|Takatsuki Domain|en|Takatsuki Domain|WD=Q10863415|lt=Takatsuki Domain|1=Takatsuki Domain}} (30,000 ''koku'') in {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|WD=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}. In 1619, his descendants were transferred to Soma; in 1627 to {{ill|Kaminoyama Domain|en|Kaminoyama Domain|WD=Q847283|lt=Kaminoyama Domain|1=Kaminoyama Domain}} in {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}}; in 1712 to {{ill|Tanaka Domain|en|Tanaka Domain|WD=Q420800|lt=Tanaka Domain|1=Tanaka Domain}} in {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}}; and finally, from 1742 to 1868 in {{ill|Numata Domain|en|Numata Domain|WD=Q10863454|lt=Numata Domain|1=Numata Domain}} (35,000 ''koku'') in ({{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}). ===Cadet branches=== Several clans claim descent from the Toki, including the {{ill|Asano clan|en|Asano clan|WD=Q2659895|lt=Asano|1=Asano clan}}, {{ill|Akechi clan|en|Akechi clan|WD=Q253992|lt=Akechi|1=Akechi clan}}, [[Seyasu clan|Seyasu]], [[Ibi clan|Ibi]], [[Hidase clan|Hidase]], [[Osu clan|Osu]], [[Twara clan|Tawara]], [[Toyama clan|Toyama]], Fumizuki and [[Funaki clan|Funaki]]. ==Select list== The first six clan heads lived in Kyoto and Settsu Tada before receiving the Mino province. The next seven clan leaders lived in Toki. Starting with Toki Yorisada, the clan heads were also the ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' of Mino Province. ===Pre-Mino ancestors=== # Emperor Seiwa (Emperor Seiwa) – the 56th Emperor of Japan # Teijun Shinoh (貞純親王) # Tsunemoto Ō (経基王) – the 6th grandson of Emperor Seiwa, Chinjufu Shogun defeated Taira Masakado # Tada Minamoto Mitsunaka (多田源満仲) – Founder of Settsu Tada Genji # {{ill|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|en|Minamoto no Yorimitsu|WD=Q590523|lt=Minamoto no Yorimitsu|1=Minamoto no Yorimitsu}} (Raikō) (源頼光) – ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', famous with Oeyama Oni Taiji and Tsuchigumo # Minamoto Yorikuni (源頼国) ===Initial Mino rulers=== # [[Minamoto no Kunifusa]] (源国房) # [[Minamoto no Mitsukuni]] (源光国) # [[Minamoto no Mitsunobu]] (源光信) # [[Minamoto no Mitsuki]] (源光基) # [[Toki Mitsuhira]] (土岐光衡) # [[Toki Mitsuyuki]] (土岐光行) # [[Toki Mitsusada]] (土岐光定) ===''Shugo'' of Mino Province=== # [[Toki Yorisada]] (土岐頼貞) (1271–1339) # [[Toki Yoritō]] (土岐頼遠) (died December 29, 1342) # [[Toki Yoriyasu]] (土岐頼康) (1318 – February 3, 1388) # [[Toki Yasuyuki]] (土岐康行) (died November 8, 1404) # [[Toki Yoritada]] (土岐頼忠) (died 1397) # [[Toki Yorimasu]] (土岐頼益) (1351–1414) # [[Toki Mochimasu]] (土岐持益) (1406–1474) # {{ill|Toki Shigeyori|en|Toki Shigeyori|WD=Q7813574|lt=Toki Shigeyori|1=Toki Shigeyori}} (土岐成頼) (1442–1497) # {{ill|Toki Masafusa|en|Toki Masafusa|WD=Q3530465|lt=Toki Masafusa|1=Toki Masafusa}} (土岐政房) (1457 – September 12, 1519) # [[Toki Yoritake]] (土岐頼武) (1488–1536) # {{ill|Toki Yorinari|en|Toki Yorinari|WD=Q837179|lt=Toki Yorinari|1=Toki Yorinari}} (土岐頼芸) (1502 – December 28, 1582) # [[Toki Yorizumi]] (土岐頼純) (1524 – December 28, 1547) # Toki Yorinari (was ''shugo'' twice) ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Toki clan]] [[de:Toki (Klan)]] [[es:Clan toki]] [[fa:خاندان توکی]] [[fr:Clan Toki]] [[it:Clan Toki]] [[ja:土岐氏]] [[pt:Clã Toki]] [[ru:Токи (клан)]] [[zh:土岐氏]] {{wikidata link|Q687635}} [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]
